other_observers: None; As far as I know, Yes I am the original finder. I don't know if anyone else saw this bird. I was birding alone.
specific_location: Along a dirt road just east of the Cottonwood Canyon picnic area and camp ground. This area is described in the birding book I was using that talked about this area. The book was "A Birder's Guide to Colorado" by Harold R. Holt, page 65.
description: I was driving through the Cottonwood Canyon area when I flushed a grayish bird with a long tail from a bush along side the road. It flew a short distance to another small tree and landed in good view. I stopped my car, got out of my car and set up my spotting scope on it. I figured I was about 40 yards away from it. Once I got the bird in view, I knew right away it was a Scissor-tailed Flycatcher. I saw what I felt was a female as its tail wasn't longer as compared to it body, maybe the same length to its body length? Still, it had a long tail that was black with white edges. Head and upper back was pale grayish in color, throat and breast was whitish, it had salmon coloring on the flanks, and I could see the reddish-salmon axillaries but only slightly because this bird had its wings folded against its body. Wings appeared dark with whitish feather edges. The bill was dark. If I had to judge the size of this bird, I'd say its body size was close to that of an Eastern Kingbird, which I have here in Wisconsin, but with a much longer tail.Behaviors: I only observed this bird for 5 minutes before moving on to do more birding. This bird just sat on a branch in this small tree looking around. I did not see it fly after I first flushed it.Call: None heardPlumage: Adult
